name: rosemary chicken pot pie
notes:
PEAS BELONG HERE: peas work well in a pot pie specifically because all the resulting gravy is consumed inside the pie — no broth is left behind on the plate, no bowl-bottom puddle. Peas are small enough to be eaten alongside the other components in every bite, they add an earthy vegetal note that the cream-rosemary-chicken base doesn't otherwise carry, and they bring more protein. They're not a standard chicken-stew addition (in a fricassee they'd get lost in a deep bowl of sauce), but the pot-pie format makes them earn their place.

POTATOES FOR STARCH WITHOUT MSG GUMMINESS: potatoes add starchy body that thickens the final filling and gives it more chew. This matters because the alternative — pushing the gravy thicker with more bouillon/MSG or more flour — leaves the filling gummy and one-note. Potatoes do the thickening structurally instead of chemically; the dish reads richer without reading artificial. Cube them small (see chopping note below).

FINELY CHOPPED, NOT PROCESSED — BITE-SIZED FOR THE GRAVY: every component (leeks, celery, carrots, potatoes, chicken) should be finely chopped but kept bite-sized — not pulverized in a food processor. The goal is a cohesive gravy solution where each forkful has a bit of everything in it. Knife-and-cutting-board instincts are right here. Processor-fine vegetables dissolve into the cream base and lose textural identity; large rustic-chunk cuts leave you fishing for specific components rather than eating a unified bite. Aim for the size that survives the bake but disappears against the others on the fork.
